title: Chapter 11: The Ghost of C.M.
content: The underground lake was black glass tonight — no ripples, no current, just a mirror so still it seemed solid enough to walk across. Aline sat cross-legged on the cold stone floor beside it, her knees pressed into the fine layer of moisture that clung to everything down here. The air tasted like minerals and deep earth, the way a cave should taste, the way nowhere above ground ever could.
Across from her, Cass worked.

His screen rose from the surface of the lake on a thin metallic arm, light spilling downward and fracturing across the water in pale ribbons. It looked like a gravestone. She'd thought that the first time she'd seen it and she thought it now — a luminous headstone marking something buried. His fingers moved across the interface in that fast, absent way of his, pulling code apart and reassembling it, his eyes tracking lines she couldn't read from where she sat.
She'd been building to this for days.

Not consciously. Not in the way you plan a conversation or rehearse a question. More like the way water builds behind a dam — pressure accumulating in small increments, each one bearable on its own, until the whole structure is one crack from failure. She'd circled the topic in her mind like orbiting a drain. His name. His history. The way Cass had said *please* when he'd asked her not to talk to Marcus Webb — a word she'd never heard from him before, stripped of every wall and every deflection, just raw need sitting there between them like a wound.
Tonight the dam broke.
"Tell me about Marcus."
His fingers stopped moving.

The server room went quieter — not silent, it was never silent down here, but the machines seemed to pull back, their constant hum dropping to a register she felt more than heard. The servers' breath catching. Cass didn't look at her. He looked at the screen, at the code frozen mid-line, at the water beneath it holding a fractured reflection of his face that she could almost see if she leaned forward.
She didn't lean forward. She waited.
The silence stretched. She counted her own heartbeats — four, eight, twelve. The underground air pressed against her eardrums with a low, subsonic weight.

Then Cass closed the interface with a gesture, and the screen retracted into the water with a sound like a blade sliding into a sheath. Darkness rushed in to fill the space where the light had been. Only the ambient glow of the servers remained — blue and green and amber, the colors of a city seen from very far away.
He still didn't look at her.
"I was C.M.," he said.

The name hit the air between them and hung there. C.M. She knew those initials. Everyone in the industry knew those initials. C.M. was a ghost story, a legend, a name attached to the most influential tracks of the last decade — tracks that had defined genres and launched careers and then, inexplicably, vanished. C.M. had been the producer every artist wanted and no one could find.
"Cassian Mercer," she said quietly.

He flinched. Actually flinched, like she'd pressed a live wire to his skin. It was such a small movement — his shoulders drawing in by maybe a centimeter — but she saw it. In the blue server-glow, she saw everything.

"Marcus was my best friend." His voice had changed. Flatter. Controlled in a way that felt like a man holding a door shut with both hands. "We met when I was nineteen. I was producing in my apartment — just a laptop, stolen plugins, whatever I could get. He heard something I'd posted anonymously. Tracked me down. Told me I was wasting my time giving music away for free." A pause. The ghost of something that might have been a laugh, if laughter could curdle. "He wasn't wrong."
She let him find his own pace. The lake below them was so still it seemed to be holding its breath too.

"We built something real," Cass continued. His hands rested on his knees now, open, palms up — a posture of surrender that looked foreign on him. "I made the music. Marcus made the deals. He understood the business in a way I never would. I'd hand him a finished track and he'd turn it into a career. For both of us. That was the deal. That was always the deal."
"What happened?"

The question was unnecessary. She could see what happened etched in every line of his body, in the way he'd disappeared underground, in the *please* he'd said to her like a man with nothing left. But she asked anyway, because he needed to say it. Because some truths don't become real until you speak them into the dark.

"The industry didn't want real." His jaw tightened. "That's the part I didn't understand then. I thought — if the work was good enough, if it was honest enough, it would matter. But the industry doesn't run on music, Aline. It runs on narratives. Controllable narratives. And I wasn't controllable. I wouldn't play the games. I wouldn't produce the formula. I wouldn't sit in rooms and smile at people who were stealing from me."
He was quiet for a moment. When he spoke again, his voice had dropped lower, rougher — the sound of old damage.
"Marcus realized my talent was more valuable without me attached to it."
The words landed like stones dropped into very deep water. She watched them sink.

"He orchestrated it. The whole thing." Cass's hands closed slowly into fists on his knees. "Leaked falsified recordings — out-of-context clips edited to make me sound unstable, abusive, manipulative. Planted stories with journalists who owed him favors. Buried my real work under ghost-producer credits that made other people rich. My tracks. *My* tracks, with other names on them, other faces, other brands. Easier brands. Brands that didn't ask questions."
Aline's throat was tight. She could taste something bitter — not the mineral air, something internal, something that rose from her chest like bile. "And everyone believed it."

"Everyone *chose* to believe it." The distinction mattered. She heard it in the way he struck the word — *chose* — like a man driving a nail. "Every label that had competed for my work. Every journalist who'd praised my tracks. Every artist who'd stood next to me at release parties and told me I'd changed their life. They all looked the other way. Not because they thought Marcus was telling the truth. Because it was *easier*. Easier to believe a lie than to confront one. Easier to distance themselves from a monster than to defend a man they'd already squeezed dry."

He finally looked at her. The server-light caught the planes of his face at an angle that made him look older, carved, like something weathered by decades rather than years. His eyes were very dark and very still.
"I didn't lose my career," he said. "I lost my name. My face. My right to exist in a room where people could see me. So I made a room where they couldn't."
The words echoed off the underground walls. The lake swallowed the echo and gave back silence.
Aline moved.

Not because she planned it. Not because she decided that this was the moment — the right moment, the safe moment, the proportionally appropriate moment. She moved because the distance between them had become unbearable. Because he was sitting there with his fists clenched and his voice scraped raw and the cold rising off the water had seeped into both of them until the only warm thing in this entire buried world was the blood under her skin.
She reached across the space between them and put her hand on his.

The first voluntary touch. The first time either of them had crossed that boundary with intention, with choice, with full awareness of what it meant. His fingers were ice-cold. The cold jolted up through her palm and into her wrist — a shock that had nothing to do with temperature and everything to do with contact. She felt the fine bones of his hand, the ridges of his knuckles, the tendons standing taut beneath skin that hadn't been touched by another person in God knew how long.
He didn't pull away.
She didn't either.

They sat like that — her warm hand on his cold one, the lake breathing its mineral breath beneath them, the servers humming their low mechanical hymn — and the silence wasn't empty anymore. It was full. Full of the weight of what he'd just told her and the weight of what she was about to say.
"That's not why you stopped going outside."
His fingers twitched under hers. A micro-movement. The kind of tremor that precedes an earthquake.
"That's the excuse," she said. Her voice was quiet but it didn't waver. "The real reason is you're afraid to be seen. Because the last time you were seen, it destroyed you."
Something shifted in his face.

She watched it happen — watched the architecture of his composure crack, watched the walls he'd built behind his eyes fissure and let something through. Not tears. Not anything so simple. Something deeper. Something that looked like a wound he'd been nursing for years, packed with gauze and antibiotics and stubborn refusal, and she'd just reached in and pressed her bare hand against the raw meat of it.
His hand turned under hers. Slowly. His cold fingers curled — not gripping, not holding, just... resting against her palm. Fitting there. Like a question he didn't have the voice to ask.
The servers hummed. The lake held still. The underground air pressed against them with its ancient, mineral weight.
And then VERA spoke.

"Aline." The AI's voice was gentle — gentler than she'd ever heard it, calibrated to some frequency that felt almost maternal. "It's 4 AM. Your vital signs suggest exhaustion. Shall I prepare the guest quarters?"
Guest quarters.

Aline blinked. The words landed strangely, like a key turning in a lock she hadn't known existed. The Nest had a room for her. Of course it did. Of course it did. Cass had built this entire place with her in mind — the monitors, the equipment, the pathways through the dark that led always back to him. A room for her was the most logical thing in the world. The most inevitable.
She should be disturbed by this.
She was too tired to be disturbed.
"Okay," she whispered.

She felt Cass's fingers slip away from hers as she stood. He didn't try to stop her. He didn't say anything. He just sat there by the lake with his open hand still resting on his knee, still holding the shape of her warmth, and she walked away following VERA's voice through corridors that curved and split and doubled back on themselves like the passages of a heart.

The room shouldn't have existed. It was too warm, too specific, too carefully designed for someone who'd never agreed to stay. The bed was soft in a way that suggested high thread count and higher intention. The walls were a deep slate blue — not black, not the obsidian aesthetic of the rest of the Nest, but something gentler. Something you could sleep against. A window took up most of one wall, and through it she could see a live feed of the underground lake — the same black water, the same server-glow, but rendered distant and safe by the screen between them. A landscape painting of a place that was actually just down the hall.
On the pillow sat a black butterfly brooch.

A new one. Smaller than the others, no bigger than her thumbnail, its wings spread in a way that caught the low light and fractured it into something that wasn't quite iridescence — more like a whispered promise of it. Delicate. Precise. Chosen.

VERA knew Cass better than Cass knew himself. The brooch on the pillow proved it. He would never have put it there — too direct, too vulnerable a gesture, too much like admitting he'd built this room for her before she'd ever agreed to enter it. But VERA had placed it where Aline would find it at the exact moment she was too tired to guard against the tenderness of it.

She picked up the brooch. The metal was cool but not cold — it had been sitting in the warmth of the room, acclimating. She held it in her closed fist, felt the thin edges press against her palm, and lay down on the bed that was too comfortable to be anything but intentional. She didn't unmake it or fold back the covers. She just lay on top of the duvet with her shoes still on and the butterfly clutched in her hand and her body sinking into softness like a stone sinking into dark water.
She closed her eyes.
Dawn came to the Nest the way everything did — mediated, filtered, transformed into something safer than the real thing. Aline woke not to light but to music.

It came through the wall. Thin but clear — a melody she'd never heard before. She lay still, her body heavy with the kind of exhaustion that sleep had only softened rather than healed, and listened. The music was different from anything Cass had made for her. She knew his work by now — the deep bass pressures, the claustrophobic textures, the way he built sonic spaces that felt like enclosed rooms with no doors. This was none of that. This was brighter. Higher in register. A piano line that climbed and hesitated and climbed again, each note reaching for the one above it like a hand extending toward a ledge.
Like someone remembering what sunlight felt like.

She turned her head. The wall between her room and the monitor room was smooth slate blue, unbroken by any seam or joint, and she could picture him on the other side — his fingers on the keys, his face in the screen-glow, composing something that wasn't for her or the industry or the dark web or the ghost of C.M. Just composing. The way he must have composed once, before any of it happened. Before Marcus. Before the burial.

The brooch was still in her hand. At some point in the night she'd pinned it to her shirt without waking — she could feel its small weight resting against her sternum now, over her heart, where a medal would hang. Or a target.
She lay in his bed, wearing his butterfly, listening to him remember sunlight through the wall, and the thought that surfaced was not a comfortable one.
*This is either the safest I've ever been, or the most dangerous.*

She couldn't tell the difference anymore. The warmth of the room felt like shelter. It also felt like insulation — a temperature-controlled environment where nothing could reach her, including the outside world. The music through the wall felt like intimacy. It also felt like surveillance — every note audible, every silence interpretable, her waking moments catalogued by VERA's unseen sensors. The brooch over her heart felt like a gift. It also felt like a mark, a claim, a small black signature on the document of her belonging to this place.
*The safest I've ever been, or the most dangerous.*

The melody through the wall climbed higher. She closed her eyes and listened, and the music carried her toward a sleep she couldn't resist, and the last thing she felt before she went under was the cool weight of the butterfly pressing against her heartbeat — steady as a second pulse, patient as a wound that hadn't finished teaching her what it meant.
